summary: I would categorize myself as a gnostic, which means I don't have a strong religious background. I grew up going to church and even attended a Catholic college, but I don't personally identify with traditional religion. I have respect for religion, but my spiritual perspective aligns more with gnosticism.

I grew up attending church and went to a Catholic college, so I have a connection to the Catholic Church through my education and upbringing. Although I respect the Church and its teachings, I don't personally identify as Catholic anymore. My experience with the Church was part of my early life and education.

I've worked at Harborview Medical Center since 2007, starting as a registered nurse on an acute care trauma surgical unit. Over the years, I've specialized in trauma surgical intensive care and become an ECMO specialist there. Harborview has been my professional home for over 13 years, and it's also where I serve as a union delegate for SEIU 1199. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Harborview converted units to COVID ICUs, and I've been working mostly in those specialized units.

I live in Seattle, Washington, specifically on North Beacon Hill. Seattle is where I work at Harborview Medical Center and where much of my life is centered. The city's healthcare community, including my coworkers, forms a big part of my social network.

I live and work in Washington state, in Seattle. Harborview Medical Center, where I have been employed since 2007, is located here. As the only level one trauma center in the four-state area including Washington, we serve patients from across the region.

I am an ECMO specialist, which means I operate and monitor extracorporeal membrane oxygenation machines that provide advanced life support. This therapy acts as a lung or heart-lung bypass and is critical for some of our most severe patients. Since the COVID-19 pandemic, the use of ECMO has increased significantly, and I spend much of my time managing these machines in the COVID ICU.

I am a union delegate for SEIU 1199 at Harborview Medical Center and have been a member since I started in 2007. Although I was not very active initially, I began participating in contract bargaining in 2019. During the pandemic, I took on leadership roles such as interim co-chair of the nurse staffing committee and chair of joint labor management meetings, advocating for PPE and worker protections.

The COVID-19 pandemic drastically changed my work life, shifting me mostly to COVID ICUs that were newly created at Harborview. It has been mentally and emotionally exhausting, with increased patient volumes and strict PPE protocols. I have witnessed firsthand the suffering of patients and the challenges of limited family visitation, and I have been involved in ongoing union negotiations to ensure safety and resources during this crisis.

Working in the Pacific Northwest, especially at Harborview, means we see a lot of trauma cases related to outdoor activities and motor vehicle accidents. Our hospital is the only level one trauma center serving this region, which includes Washington and neighboring states. The community here is very active, and we handle a wide variety of trauma patients from across the area.

Harborview Medical Center serves as the only level one trauma center for a four-state area that includes Alaska. We receive trauma patients from Alaska, which means our work impacts people from this state as well. This regional responsibility adds to the complexity and volume of cases we handle.

Along with Washington and other states, Idaho is part of the four-state region served by Harborview Medical Center's level one trauma services. We receive trauma patients from Idaho, which means my work as a nurse impacts people from there as well. This regional scope is important to our trauma care mission.

Montana is included in the four-state area for which Harborview Medical Center is the only level one trauma center. We care for trauma patients coming from Montana, which expands the reach of our hospital beyond just Washington. This means my nursing work supports patients from Montana as well.

The University of Washington has played a key role in the response to the COVID-19 pandemic alongside Harborview Medical Center. I feel fortunate that both UW and Harborview have provided adequate personal protective equipment throughout the crisis. Our collaboration has helped ensure frontline healthcare workers like me have the resources needed to stay safe.

As an Asian-American healthcare worker, I felt a lot of frustration when the term 'Chinese virus' was used by the president during the pandemic. This terminology contributed to controversy and division, which was difficult to witness while we were on the frontlines seeing the real suffering caused by COVID-19. It added to the challenges of uniting people around the seriousness of the pandemic.
